sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Excellent! MOUSE on the green block changed gameplay (gameplay_changed=True) and increased the green block count from 13 to 15! This is a major discovery.
Looking at the image, the layout has changed significantly. The green blocks and blue regions have shifted. The character is still at the same position.
So MOUSE on green blocks changes the board layout! This is the key mechanic. The goal is likely to use MOUSE to click on green blocks to transform the board until the level is complete.
Let me explore this mechanic more. I need to understand what clicking on green blocks does and what the goal state looks like.
Let me try clicking on different green blocks to see the pattern.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# MOUSE on green blocks changes gameplay! Green count went from 13 to 15.
# Let me explore this mechanic by clicking on different green blocks

# First, let me see the current green block positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Current green blocks:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  N: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, hash={n['hash'][:8]}")

# Try clicking on a different green block
# Let me click on one at rows 7-11, cols 13-17
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 9, 'col': 15}])
print(f"\nMOUSE on another green: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
green_count = sum(1 for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N')
print(f"Green blocks: {green_count}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  N: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
